title: Space Tech Briefing — June 25, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space cadets and future pioneers! This is your daily dose of cosmic currents, live from the Space Technology Command Center. It's Thursday, June 25, 2026, and we're kicking off your day with a jolt of orbital excitement and Earth-bound insights!

First up, mark your calendars! France is gearing up for a celestial spectacle. The 2026 edition of the "Nights of the Stars" — *Nuits des étoiles* — is set to illuminate the skies across the country from August 7th to the 9th. Co-organized by the French Astronomy Association, Planète Sciences, and our friends at CNES, this annual event invites everyone to gaze upwards and explore the wonders of our universe. Get ready to connect with the cosmos, stargazers!

Now, let's talk about our home planet, where space technology is playing a crucial role in understanding its mighty forces. The El Niño phenomenon is currently taking shape in the Pacific Ocean, and scientists are watching it closely. Oceanography satellites are actively studying the early signs, providing vital data to predict its intensity and potential global impact. This is a powerful reminder of how our eyes in the sky are essential tools for climate forecasting and protecting communities worldwide.

And finally, a thrilling update from the innovative minds at SpaceX! They've just unveiled a groundbreaking new project called "Starfall." This is an uncrewed, recoverable cargo capsule designed to bring payloads safely back to Earth from orbit. The Starfall Demo Mission successfully launched on Wednesday, June 23rd, aboard a Falcon 9 rocket from Cape Canaveral, heading to low Earth orbit. While SpaceX hasn't specified the exact number of payloads, this mission is a crucial step in demonstrating the vehicle's capability to return valuable cargo, opening up incredible possibilities for future space logistics!

What an incredible morning for space exploration and Earth observation! That's all for your Space Technology Morning Briefing today, June 25, 2026. Stay curious, keep looking up, and we'll catch you back here tomorrow for more groundbreaking news. Have a stellar day, everyone!
